instruction: Explain TypeScript mapped types and show how to build utility types like Partial, Required, and custom mapped types.
output:
<think>
Mapped types are one of TypeScript's most powerful features for type-level programming. They iterate over the keys of a type and transform each property. The syntax is [K in keyof T]: TransformedType. The built-in Partial<T> adds ? to every property, making them optional. Required<T> removes ? with -?. Readonly<T> adds readonly. But you can build much more sophisticated transformations: making only certain properties optional, transforming value types, filtering properties by type, and combining multiple transformations. The key modifiers are + and - which add or remove readonly and ? modifiers. When combined with conditional types and template literal types, mapped types become a full type-level programming language. Understanding mapped types is essential for building type-safe APIs, ORM models, form libraries, and any code that needs to derive types from other types.
</think>

```typescript
// === MAPPED TYPES: Transform every property of a type ===

interface User {
  id: number;
  name: string;
  email: string;
  isAdmin: boolean;
}

// How Partial<T> works internally:
type MyPartial<T> = {
  [K in keyof T]?: T[K];  // Add ? to each property
};

// How Required<T> works:
type MyRequired<T> = {
  [K in keyof T]-?: T[K];  // Remove ? with -?
};

// How Readonly<T> works:
type MyReadonly<T> = {
  readonly [K in keyof T]: T[K];  // Add readonly
};

// Mutable<T> — remove readonly
type Mutable<T> = {
  -readonly [K in keyof T]: T[K];  // -readonly removes it
};

// === CUSTOM MAPPED TYPES ===

// Make only specific keys optional
type PartialBy<T, K extends keyof T> = Omit<T, K> & Partial<Pick<T, K>>;

type CreateUserInput = PartialBy<User, 'id' | 'isAdmin'>;
// { name: string; email: string; id?: number; isAdmin?: boolean }

// Transform all value types
type Nullable<T> = {
  [K in keyof T]: T[K] | null;
};

type NullableUser = Nullable<User>;
// { id: number | null; name: string | null; ... }

// Getters for all properties
type Getters<T> = {
  [K in keyof T as `get${Capitalize<string & K>}`]: () => T[K];
};

type UserGetters = Getters<User>;
// { getId: () => number; getName: () => string; ... }

// Filter properties by type
type StringKeys<T> = {
  [K in keyof T as T[K] extends string ? K : never]: T[K];
};

type UserStrings = StringKeys<User>;
// { name: string; email: string } — only string properties

// Event map from interface
type EventMap<T> = {
  [K in keyof T as `on${Capitalize<string & K>}Change`]: (value: T[K]) => void;
};

type UserEvents = EventMap<User>;
// { onIdChange: (value: number) => void; onNameChange: (value: string) => void; ... }

// === PRACTICAL: Form state type ===

type FormState<T> = {
  values: T;
  errors: { [K in keyof T]?: string };
  touched: { [K in keyof T]?: boolean };
  dirty: { [K in keyof T]?: boolean };
};

function createForm<T>(initial: T): FormState<T> {
  return {
    values: { ...initial },
    errors: {},
    touched: {},
    dirty: {},
  };
}

const form = createForm<User>({
  id: 0,
  name: '',
  email: '',
  isAdmin: false,
});

form.errors.name = 'Required';    // ✅ string | undefined
form.touched.email = true;         // ✅ boolean | undefined
// form.errors.foo = 'x';          // ❌ 'foo' not in keyof User
```

instruction: How do template literal types work in TypeScript? Show practical examples of type-safe string manipulation at the type level.
output:
<think>
Template literal types, introduced in TypeScript 4.1, bring string manipulation to the type system. They use the same backtick syntax as JavaScript template literals but operate on types. You can concatenate string literal types, extract parts of strings, and build complex string patterns — all at compile time. Combined with conditional types and mapped types, they enable type-safe routing, event systems, CSS-in-JS libraries, and API clients. The built-in intrinsic types Uppercase, Lowercase, Capitalize, and Uncapitalize transform string literal types. Template literals can also be used in union distributions — if you have a union type in a template position, TypeScript distributes over it, creating all possible combinations. This is incredibly powerful for generating exhaustive type unions from simpler types, like generating all possible CSS property-value pairs or all valid API endpoint paths.
</think>

```typescript
// === BASIC: String type concatenation ===

type Greeting = `Hello, ${string}!`;
const g1: Greeting = 'Hello, World!';  // ✅
// const g2: Greeting = 'Hi, World!';  // ❌ Must start with "Hello, "

// === UNION DISTRIBUTION: Generates all combinations ===

type Method = 'GET' | 'POST' | 'PUT' | 'DELETE';
type Resource = 'users' | 'posts' | 'comments';

type Endpoint = `${Lowercase<Method>}_${Resource}`;
// 'get_users' | 'get_posts' | 'get_comments' | 'post_users' | ... (12 total)

// === INTRINSIC STRING TYPES ===

type Upper = Uppercase<'hello'>;      // 'HELLO'
type Lower = Lowercase<'HELLO'>;      // 'hello'
type Cap = Capitalize<'hello'>;        // 'Hello'
type Uncap = Uncapitalize<'Hello'>;    // 'hello'

// === PRACTICAL: Type-safe event system ===

type EventName<T> = {
  [K in keyof T & string as `on${Capitalize<K>}`]: (value: T[K]) => void;
};

interface AppState {
  count: number;
  message: string;
  loading: boolean;
}

type AppEvents = EventName<AppState>;
// {
//   onCount: (value: number) => void;
//   onMessage: (value: string) => void;
//   onLoading: (value: boolean) => void;
// }

// === PRACTICAL: Type-safe CSS properties ===

type CSSUnit = 'px' | 'em' | 'rem' | '%' | 'vh' | 'vw';
type CSSValue = `${number}${CSSUnit}`;

function setSize(width: CSSValue, height: CSSValue): void {
  console.log(`${width} x ${height}`);
}

setSize('100px', '50vh');    // ✅
// setSize('100', '50vh');   // ❌ Missing unit
// setSize('100xyz', '50vh'); // ❌ Invalid unit

// === ADVANCED: Extract parts of string types ===

type ExtractRoute<T extends string> =
  T extends `/${infer Segment}/${infer Rest}`
    ? Segment | ExtractRoute<`/${Rest}`>
    : T extends `/${infer Last}`
      ? Last
      : never;

type Segments = ExtractRoute<'/api/users/profile'>;
// 'api' | 'users' | 'profile'

// === ADVANCED: Parse route params ===

type RouteParams<T extends string> =
  T extends `${string}:${infer Param}/${infer Rest}`
    ? { [K in Param | keyof RouteParams<Rest>]: string }
    : T extends `${string}:${infer Param}`
      ? { [K in Param]: string }
      : {};

type UserRouteParams = RouteParams<'/users/:userId/posts/:postId'>;
// { userId: string; postId: string }

function navigate<T extends string>(
  route: T,
  params: RouteParams<T>
): string {
  let path: string = route;
  for (const [key, value] of Object.entries(params)) {
    path = path.replace(`:${key}`, value as string);
  }
  return path;
}

const url = navigate('/users/:userId/posts/:postId', {
  userId: '42',
  postId: '100',
  // extra: '1'  // ❌ Not a valid param
});
// Result: '/users/42/posts/100'
```

instruction: Explain the TypeScript satisfies operator and const type parameters. When should you use each?
output:
<think>
The satisfies operator (TypeScript 4.9+) and const type parameters (TypeScript 5.0+) solve different but related problems around type inference. The satisfies operator validates that an expression matches a type WITHOUT widening the inferred type. Before satisfies, you had two choices: type annotation (which widens and loses specific literal types) or no annotation (which gives narrow types but no validation). satisfies gives you both — validation against a constraint AND preservation of the narrow inferred type. Const type parameters solve the problem where generic functions infer wide types. Normally, if you pass ['a', 'b'] to a function expecting T[], TypeScript infers T as string[]. With const T, it infers readonly ['a', 'b'] — the narrowest possible type, preserving literal values and tuple structure. This is equivalent to the caller writing as const but controlled by the function author.
</think>

```typescript
// === SATISFIES: Validate without widening ===

type Color = 'red' | 'green' | 'blue';
type ColorMap = Record<Color, string | number[]>;

// Option 1: Type annotation — validates but WIDENS
const colors1: ColorMap = {
  red: '#ff0000',
  green: [0, 255, 0],
  blue: '#0000ff',
};
// colors1.red is string | number[] — lost the specific type!
// colors1.red.toUpperCase()  // ❌ Error: might be number[]

// Option 2: No annotation — narrow but UNVALIDATED
const colors2 = {
  red: '#ff0000',
  green: [0, 255, 0],
  blue: '#0000ff',
  // yellow: '#ffff00',  // No error! Not validated against ColorMap
};

// Option 3: SATISFIES — validates AND keeps narrow types
const colors3 = {
  red: '#ff0000',
  green: [0, 255, 0],
  blue: '#0000ff',
  // yellow: '#ffff00',  // ❌ Error: 'yellow' not in Color
} satisfies ColorMap;

colors3.red.toUpperCase();     // ✅ TypeScript knows it's string
colors3.green.map(x => x);     // ✅ TypeScript knows it's number[]
// Best of both worlds: validated + narrow type preserved

// === PRACTICAL: Config validation ===

interface Config {
  port: number;
  host: string;
  features: Record<string, boolean>;
}

const config = {
  port: 3000,
  host: 'localhost',
  features: {
    darkMode: true,
    betaFeatures: false,
  },
} satisfies Config;

// config.port is number (not widened)
// config.features.darkMode is boolean (specific key known)

// === CONST TYPE PARAMETERS (TS 5.0+) ===

// Without const: T inferred as wide type
function routes<T extends readonly string[]>(paths: T): T {
  return paths;
}
const r1 = routes(['home', 'about', 'contact']);
// r1: string[] — lost the literal types!

// With const: T inferred as narrow literal tuple
function routesConst<const T extends readonly string[]>(paths: T): T {
  return paths;
}
const r2 = routesConst(['home', 'about', 'contact']);
// r2: readonly ['home', 'about', 'contact'] — literals preserved!

// === PRACTICAL: Type-safe builder with const ===

function definePermissions<const T extends Record<string, readonly string[]>>(
  permissions: T
): T {
  return permissions;
}

const perms = definePermissions({
  admin: ['read', 'write', 'delete'],
  viewer: ['read'],
  editor: ['read', 'write'],
});

// perms.admin is readonly ['read', 'write', 'delete'] — not string[]
type AdminPerm = (typeof perms.admin)[number];  // 'read' | 'write' | 'delete'
type ViewerPerm = (typeof perms.viewer)[number]; // 'read'

// Without const, these would all be string — useless

// === COMBINING: satisfies + const ===

type RouteConfig = Record<string, { path: string; auth: boolean }>;

const appRoutes = {
  home: { path: '/', auth: false },
  dashboard: { path: '/dashboard', auth: true },
  settings: { path: '/settings', auth: true },
} satisfies RouteConfig;

// Validated against RouteConfig AND narrow types preserved
type RouteName = keyof typeof appRoutes; // 'home' | 'dashboard' | 'settings'
```

instruction: Show how recursive generic types work in TypeScript. Build a deeply nested type-safe accessor and a JSON type.
output:
<think>
Recursive generic types allow types to reference themselves, enabling tree structures, nested objects, and deep type inference. The classic example is a JSON type — JSON values can be strings, numbers, booleans, null, arrays of JSON values, or objects with JSON values. This naturally recursive definition maps directly to a recursive TypeScript type. Another powerful use is deep path accessors — given a nested object type, you can build a type that represents all valid dot-separated paths through it, and a function that returns the correct type for each path. The key challenge with recursive types is avoiding infinite recursion — TypeScript has depth limits and will error if recursion goes too deep. You need base cases to terminate recursion. Template literal types combined with recursive types enable dot-path string types. Conditional types with infer enable extracting nested property types given a path.
</think>

```typescript
// === RECURSIVE JSON TYPE ===

type JsonValue =
  | string
  | number
  | boolean
  | null
  | JsonValue[]           // Array of JSON values (recursive)
  | { [key: string]: JsonValue };  // Object of JSON values (recursive)

function parseJson(text: string): JsonValue {
  return JSON.parse(text);
}

const data: JsonValue = {
  name: 'Alice',
  age: 30,
  scores: [95, 87, 92],     // JsonValue[]
  address: {                  // Nested object
    city: 'Portland',
    coords: [45.5, -122.6],  // Nested array
  },
};

// === DEEP PATH TYPE: All valid dot-paths through an object ===

type DeepPaths<T, Prefix extends string = ''> = T extends object
  ? {
      [K in keyof T & string]: T[K] extends object
        ? `${Prefix}${K}` | DeepPaths<T[K], `${Prefix}${K}.`>
        : `${Prefix}${K}`;
    }[keyof T & string]
  : never;

interface AppConfig {
  server: {
    host: string;
    port: number;
    ssl: {
      enabled: boolean;
      cert: string;
    };
  };
  database: {
    url: string;
    pool: number;
  };
}

type ConfigPath = DeepPaths<AppConfig>;
// 'server' | 'server.host' | 'server.port' | 'server.ssl' |
// 'server.ssl.enabled' | 'server.ssl.cert' | 'database' |
// 'database.url' | 'database.pool'

// === DEEP GET: Type-safe nested property access ===

type DeepGet<T, Path extends string> =
  Path extends `${infer Head}.${infer Tail}`
    ? Head extends keyof T
      ? DeepGet<T[Head], Tail>
      : never
    : Path extends keyof T
      ? T[Path]
      : never;

function deepGet<T, P extends DeepPaths<T>>(
  obj: T,
  path: P
): DeepGet<T, P> {
  const keys = (path as string).split('.');
  let current: any = obj;
  for (const key of keys) {
    current = current[key];
  }
  return current;
}

const config: AppConfig = {
  server: { host: 'localhost', port: 8080, ssl: { enabled: true, cert: '/cert.pem' } },
  database: { url: 'postgres://localhost/db', pool: 10 },
};

const host = deepGet(config, 'server.host');        // string
const port = deepGet(config, 'server.port');        // number
const sslEnabled = deepGet(config, 'server.ssl.enabled'); // boolean
// deepGet(config, 'server.invalid');                // ❌ Compile error

// === RECURSIVE TREE TYPE ===

interface TreeNode<T> {
  value: T;
  children: TreeNode<T>[];  // Recursive reference
}

function sumTree(node: TreeNode<number>): number {
  return node.value + node.children.reduce(
    (acc, child) => acc + sumTree(child),
    0
  );
}

const tree: TreeNode<number> = {
  value: 1,
  children: [
    { value: 2, children: [] },
    {
      value: 3,
      children: [
        { value: 4, children: [] },
      ],
    },
  ],
};

console.log(sumTree(tree)); // 10

// === RECURSIVE READONLY DEEP ===

type DeepReadonly<T> = T extends (infer U)[]
  ? readonly DeepReadonly<U>[]
  : T extends object
    ? { readonly [K in keyof T]: DeepReadonly<T[K]> }
    : T;

type FrozenConfig = DeepReadonly<AppConfig>;
// All nested properties are readonly — can't modify anything
```
